title: Heptapleurum insculptum
intro: Heptapleurum insculptum, also known as Tī kōuka, is a New Zealand native tree species belonging to the Araliaceae family. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments such as skin conditions, respiratory issues, and digestive problems. The bioactive constituents of Heptapleurum insculptum include lignans, phenolic acids, and saponins, which have been identified as responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional Māori medicine, the plant is often used to make infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health conditions.
taxonomy: Heptapleurum insculptum is classified as a species of plant in the family Araliaceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the ginseng or ivy family. It is native to the tropical regions of Australia and New Guinea. The plant is also known as the "copper ivy" due to its distinctive copper-colored leaves. Heptapleurum insculptum is further classified as a member of the genus Heptapleurum.

In traditional medicine, Heptapleurum insculptum is used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions.
